does: This paper presents Phoenix-VL 1.5 Medium, a 123-billion-parameter multimodal and multilingual foundation model built through continued pretraining of Mistral Medium 3.1 on a localized 1-trillion-token corpus covering visual and textual content, followed by a 250-billion-token long-context extension phase. Post-training incorporated 22 billion tokens from human-annotated Singapore-specific multimodal datasets and curated text on Singapore culture, legislation, and knowledge, supplemented by 5 billion tokens of alignment via Online Direct Preference Optimization. The model achieves state-of-the-art results on Singapore-focused benchmarks covering multimodal understanding, legal text, and government policy while maintaining competitive global performance on general multimodal, multilingual, and STEM tasks, demonstrating that regional domain adaptation can proceed with minimal degradation to broad capabilities.
connects: This work extends the emerging paradigm of adapting large pretrained foundation models to specific geographic and cultural contexts, building on recent successes in continued pretraining and domain-specific fine-tuning of models like Mistral. It combines contemporary techniques in long-context extension and Online Direct Preference Optimization for alignment, drawing from recent advances in preference-based training methods. The approach responds to growing interest in developing sovereign AI assets and regional language models, addressing limitations of globally-trained models on localized content. It engages with prior work on multimodal pretraining at scale, multilingual capabilities in large models, and evaluation methodologies for specialized domains, particularly legal and policy understanding.
enables: This work opens pathways for developing localized foundation models serving specific geographic regions, demonstrating that domain adaptation need not sacrifice global competitiveness. It enables downstream applications in Singapore's legal, government, and cultural sectors with models tailored to local context. The approach provides a template for other nations and regions seeking sovereign AI assets and establishes benchmarks for evaluating regional model performance. Future work can build on this framework to explore trade-offs between domain specialization and generalization, extend the methodology to other languages and regions, develop more efficient domain adaptation techniques, and create larger multimodal corpora for underrepresented regions, advancing the accessibility and utility of AI systems across diverse geographic and cultural contexts.
